---
title: "Week 8 — Planning and Allocation"
description: "Combining LP with input-output to formulate the Cockshott-Kantorovich planning problem."
order: 8
---

## Framing

This is the keystone week. You combine LP (Week 6) with input-output analysis (Week 7) to formulate the full economic planning problem that Kantorovich and then Cockshott/Cottrell have worked on. Everything you've done feeds in here.

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal" collapse="true" .author-notes}
## Author's notes for this chapter

- [Reading notes]({{< var notes-base >}}/the_calculation_course/weeks/week-08-planning/reading-notes)
- [Open questions]({{< var notes-base >}}/the_calculation_course/weeks/week-08-planning/questions)
- [Insights and connections]({{< var notes-base >}}/the_calculation_course/weeks/week-08-planning/insights)
- [References to revisit]({{< var notes-base >}}/the_calculation_course/weeks/week-08-planning/refs)
- [Scratchpad notebook](https://github.com/jquacinella/EconomicPlanningCourse/blob/main/courses/the_calculation_course/notes/weeks/week-08-planning/scratchpad.ipynb)
:::

## Learning objectives

Set up a multi-sector planning problem: given technology A, labor coefficients v, labor endowment L̄, and a desired composition of final demand, find the plan that maximizes scale (or utility of a final-demand bundle) subject to resource constraints. Compute planning prices as LP dual variables. Contrast quantity-targeting with price-targeting. Understand the Cockshott-Cottrell "labour-time" proposal and its difference from conventional shadow-price approaches. Distinguish full *Naturalrechnung* (Neurath: no scalar reduction) from labor-time-monist planning (Cockshott: one common scalar but no money), and recognize that the multi-resource LP variant of the planning problem is technically a Neurathian formulation — each additional resource constraint adds a dimension to the dual-price vector, and the planner chooses an objective only by selecting which dimensions to scalarize. Understand why planning is computationally tractable at realistic scale.

## Core concepts

The planning LP: max α s.t. (I − A)x ≥ α·d*, v·x ≤ L̄, x ≥ 0 (where d* is a desired basket and α is the scale). Dual variables — planning prices — are labor values when the constraint is labor. Von Neumann balanced growth: the maximal λ such that the economy can produce (λ · consumption-bundle) next period from this period's output. Iterative plan-adjustment algorithms. Planning *in natura* in two distinct senses. (i) Neurath's full *Naturalrechnung*: no scalar reduction; the plan is a vector of physical quantities chosen against multiple physical constraints, and trade-offs between non-commensurable goods (labor-time vs energy vs land vs emissions) are resolved by political/deliberative procedure rather than by reduction to a single numéraire. (ii) Cockshott's labor-time-monist *in natura*: scalar reduction to one number — labor hours — but no monetary prices. The two are distinct positions; Cockshott is sometimes described as a Neurathian, but the labor-time monism is a real concession to Mises that Neurath himself never made. The full Neurath–Cockshott contrast and the multi-criteria optimization formalism are treated in [Appendix D](../appendices/appendix-d-calculation-in-kind.qmd). The calculation debate — why 20th-century arguments against planning (Mises, Hayek) are computational claims that modern hardware and algorithms refute.

## Resource schedule

- Day 1: Read *Mathematics to Plan an Economy* (cibcom.org PDF): <https://cibcom.org/wp-content/uploads/2022/06/Mathematics_to_plan_an_economy_.pdf>. This is a tight, mathematically explicit introduction to what we're about to build.
- Day 2: Cockshott & Cottrell, *Towards a New Socialism*, Chapter 3 (*Information and Economics*) and Chapter 9 (*Planning*). Free PDF: <https://users.wfu.edu/cottrell/socialism_book/new_socialism.pdf>. Read for the architecture of the proposal, not the polemics. Note in particular the argument about computational complexity being ~ N log N.
- Day 3: Re-read Cockshott's *Returning to Kantorovich* blog post — now the LP details are inside you. Run his three-sector example (energy, food, machines with labor, wind, river, fertile and highland land) in PuLP or cvxpy. Pair with O'Neill, "In Partial Praise of a Positivist: The Work of Otto Neurath" (*Radical Philosophy* 74, 1995), free online — a 12-page article that reconstructs Neurath specifically as a planning theorist and is the best single secondary source for the connection between *Naturalrechnung* and modern multi-resource LP. (Cockshott's three-sector example with multiple land types is, structurally, a small Neurathian plan: each land type is a separate physical resource constraint with its own dual price.)
- Day 4: Paul Cockshott's YouTube lecture series *Marx, Materialism, Mathematics, and Computing* — Lectures 1 and 5 are especially relevant. Lecture 1: <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-u1IbJPdL0c>. Lecture 5: <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FcZGvmjI9Vw>.
- Day 5: Briefly read QuantEcon's *Von Neumann Growth Model* lecture: <https://python.quantecon.org/von_neumann_model.html>. This is the most general LP formulation of a growing economy and shadows Morishima's later chapters.
- Day 6: Project.

## Mini project — Three-sector planning model

Build a three-sector economy (agriculture, industry, services) with a technical matrix A of your choosing (plausible positive entries, Perron-Frobenius eigenvalue < 1), labor coefficients v, and a total labor endowment of, say, 1000 hours. Specify a target final-demand composition d* = [0.5, 0.3, 0.2] (shares). Using cvxpy, solve the planning LP: maximize α subject to (I − A)x ≥ α·d*, v·x ≤ 1000, x ≥ 0. Report x*, α* (the scale of the standard basket you can deliver), and the dual variables on the labor constraint (= labor value in hours of one "unit of the standard basket"). Then do three things: (1) halve the technical coefficient A[1,2] (industry becomes 50% less agriculture-intensive) and re-solve; report how α* and the plan change. (2) Add a second resource constraint (say energy): w·x ≤ 500. Re-solve. When you do this, you have written down a *Naturalrechnung* — two resource dimensions, no scalar reduction at the constraint level. The dual variables are now a 2-vector of shadow prices: hours-per-unit and kWh-per-unit. There is no logical move that collapses these into one number; any such collapse is an additional choice (an assumed exchange rate, a political weighting, a market price). Note this. Most of the rest of the controversy literature *implicitly* makes that additional choice without naming it as a choice. (3) Change d* to favor services more heavily and comment on what happens.

## Self-check

Why is this LP the computational equivalent of a market? What does the dual variable on the labor constraint represent, and why does it deserve the name "labor value"? What does the dual variable on a specific output constraint ((I − A)x ≥ αd*) represent? If your planning LP has three resource constraints (labor, energy, land), you have three shadow prices. What logical operation would let you collapse them into one number, and is that operation available within the LP itself? If not, what kind of operation is it? Cockshott argues the computational complexity of national planning is ~ N log N — where does this come from, and do you believe it?

## Bridge

Everything so far has been static — a snapshot. Week 9 introduces dynamics via differential equations, so that by Week 10 you can talk about growth, stability, and how economies *change over time* — the core questions of Morishima's later work.
